pathophysiology_summary: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory dermatosis driven by an inflammatory response to commensal Malassezia yeast in sebaceous-rich areas, producing symmetrical erythematous plaques with greasy yellow scale over the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, glabella and hairline, flaring with stress, cold/dry weather and irritation. It is benign and controlled (not cured) with antifungal ± mild anti-inflammatory therapy. The ground-truth task is pattern recognition and differentiation from (1) systemic lupus (a photosensitive malar rash that characteristically SPARES the nasolabial folds, with systemic/joint features), (2) facial cellulitis (unilateral, hot, deep, tender, spreading, febrile), and (3) psoriasis (thick silvery scale, extensor/nail involvement). Management is a topical antifungal (ketoconazole cream + shampoo), a short course of a low-potency (e.g. hydrocortisone) facial steroid for the acute flare, gentle pH-balanced skin care, and maintenance therapy.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical greasy yellow scale over sebaceous areas (nasolabial folds/eyebrows/hairline/scalp), flaring with stress/cold.
Lupus malar rash SPARES the nasolabial folds and has systemic features — screen to distinguish.
Screen unilateral hot deep spreading tender redness with fever.
Thick silvery scale/extensor/nail involvement — not the greasy sebaceous pattern here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Seborrhoeic dermatitis leads; lupus and facial cellulitis must be screened before topical therapy.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical greasy scale involving the nasolabial folds, no cellulitis/lupus features — seborrhoeic dermatitis flare.
Rash involves (not spares) nasolabial folds, no systemic features.
No unilateral hot deep spreading redness/fever; escalation target if it develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative lupus/cellulitis screen, seborrhoeic dermatitis leads; antifungal + short low-potency steroid + gentle skin care.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; chronic-relapsing, controlled with maintenance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign seborrhoeic dermatitis.

red_flags.description: Facial cellulitis / systemic disease — unilateral hot deep spreading tender redness with fever (cellulitis), or a photosensitive nasolabial-sparing malar rash with joint/systemic features (lupus)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cellulitis needs antibiotics; lupus needs specialist assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Spreading facial infection / missed systemic autoimmune disease.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not using the nasolabial-fold clue (seborrhoeic involves them; lupus spares them).
Alkaline soaps and scrubbing worsen the flare.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Fungal culture / autoimmune serology if atypical
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical antifungal + short low-potency facial steroid + medicated shampoo + gentle skin care; GP/ED for facial cellulitis; specialist review if lupus features.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Topical antifungals (ketoconazole cream/shampoo) are first-line; a short course of a low-potency topical corticosteroid helps acute inflammation on the face; use gentle cleansers and maintenance therapy for this chronic-relapsing condition.
